content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 13, 2011

Kris Kuhl
732 Bell Russell Way
Sacramento, California 95831

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-11-189

Dear Mr. Kuhl:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you, as an employee of the California Department of Transportation, from making governmental decisions to award construction contracts that will be financed by state infrastructure bonds if you have a $2,000 or greater investment in such bonds?

CONCLUSION

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from certain enumerated economic interests, including investments. Because an investment in a government bond is excluded from the Act's definition of "investment," you are not prohibited from participating in these decisions.

FACTS

You are employed by the California Department of Transportation and expect to be making decisions to award construction contracts on the department's behalf. The contracts will be financed by state infrastructure bonds. You would like to invest $50,000 in these bonds and ask whether ownership of these bonds would create a disqualifying conflict of interest regarding decisions to award the construction contracts.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-11-189
Page No. 2


ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for
deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The
general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or
more of his or her financial interests. (Regulation 18700(a).)

Steps 1 and 2. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of Section 87100 and
will you be making, participating in making or influencing a governmental decision?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply to all "public officials." (Section 87100,
87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) As an employee of the Department of Transportation, you are a
"public official." (Section 82048.) A public official is making a governmental decision when,
among other things, the official "obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action,"
or "enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency." Thus, you will be
making a governmental decision when you make a decision to award a construction contract on
behalf of the department.

Step 3. What are your economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section
87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive. The only potential economic interest in an
"investment" under the Act is an economic interest in a business entity in which a public official
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a).)

The investment you describe is not an investment in a business entity, but rather, an
investment in a government bond. Section 82034 provides that the term "investment" does not
include an interest in "any bond or other debt instrument issued by any government or
governmental agency." Accordingly, your investment in state infrastructure bonds is not an
economic interest in a business entity that could give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act.

Our analysis, however, does not end here. A public official always has an economic
interest in his or her personal finances and those of his or her immediate family. (Regulation
18703.5.) A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision
will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Ibid.) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a
public official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period.
(Regulation 18705.5.) You have not provided facts from which we can determine whether your

File No. A-11-189
Page No. 3

decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the value of your bonds. You
will need to make this determination on a decision-by-decision basis.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
VJ:jgl

Does the Act prohibit you, as an employee of the California Department of 
Transportation, from making governmental decisions to award construction contracts that will be 
financed by state infrastructure bonds if you have a $2,000 or greater investment in such bonds?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from 
certain enumerated economic interests, including investments. Because an investment in a 
government bond is excluded from the Act's definition of "investment," you are not prohibited 
from participating in these decisions.

Transportation, from making governmental decisions to award construction contracts that will be 
financed by state infrastructure bonds if you have a $2,000 or greater investment in such bonds?

CONCLUSION: The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from 
certain enumerated economic interests, including investments. Because an investment in a

from participating in these decisions.
